cards.back: x_{k+1}=x_k-f(x_k)/f'(x_k). It is obtained by taking the x-axis intercept of the tangent line at the current iterate.
A first-order Taylor expansion of f around the current guess, with higher-order terms neglected.

cards.front: What is the main geometric idea behind Newton-Raphson?
cards.back: Draw the tangent line to the curve at x_k and use the tangent's x-axis crossing as the next estimate of the root.

cards.front: How does the secant method differ from Newton-Raphson?
cards.back: It replaces the exact derivative with a finite slope computed from the two most recent iterates, making it derivative-free.

cards.front: Write the general fixed-point iteration form.
cards.back: x_{k+1}=g(x_k). If the sequence converges, the limit x* must satisfy x*=g(x*).

cards.front: How can a root-finding problem be turned into a fixed-point problem?
cards.back: Choose a mapping like g(x)=x-mu f(x). Then fixed points of g correspond to roots of f(x)=0 when mu is nonzero.

cards.front: What is the local slope condition for fixed-point convergence?
cards.back: A standard sufficient local condition is |g'(x)|<1 near the fixed point, so the mapping acts like a contraction.

cards.front: For f(x)=ax+b and x_{k+1}=x_k-mu(ax_k+b), what range of mu gives convergence?
cards.back: The condition is |1-mu a|<1, which gives 0<mu<2/a when a is positive.

cards.front: Why is Newton-Raphson considered a local method?
cards.back: Its fast convergence depends on being sufficiently close to the root. From a poor starting point, the tangent-line jump can move the sequence away from the target.

cards.front: Why can two fixed-point rearrangements of the same equation behave differently?
cards.back: Because convergence depends on the chosen mapping g(x), especially its slope and whether it maps the chosen interval into itself, not only on the underlying root.
